coredata.dc:title: Effect of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) on the mechanical, dynamic, and durability properties of concrete
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Raj, Anand
Yamkasikorn, Papassara
Wangtawesap, Ratabhat
Win, Thwe Thwe
Ngamkhanong, Chayut
Jongvivatsakul, Pitcha
Prasittisopin, Lapyote
Panpranot, Joongjai
Kaewunruen, Sakdirat
coredata.dc:description: Addressing ecological concerns stemming from concrete usage is paramount, prompting exploration into additives for mitigation. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been recognized for their potential to improve the mechanical attributes of cement composites. Additionally, the electrochemical reduction of a saturated solution of carbon dioxide (CO2) and monoethanolamine (CO2-MEA) effectively removes CO2 from the atmosphere. This study delves into the influence of GQDs on the mechanical and durability aspects of concrete. It is noted that the control mix of concrete without GQDs was specifically designed for concrete railway sleepers. Varied proportions of GQDs, ranging from 0.3 % to 1.2 % at 0.3 % increments, were incorporated to assess their impact on fresh and hardened concrete properties. Mechanical properties such as compressive strength, splitting tensile strength, flexural strength, and dynamic properties were evaluated. Durability assessments encompassing water absorption and chloride ion penetration were conducted. Microstructural analysis via Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) imaging and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elucidated the concrete's internal composition. Notably, an optimal GQDs percentage of 0.3 % was observed, signifying its efficacy in enhancing the performance of concrete. When 0.3 % of GQDs was added to concrete, compressive strength, split tensile strength, and flexural strength were enhanced by 10.8 %, 23 %, and 11 % respectively. The incorporation of GQDs resulted in a notable improvement in the fundamental frequencies and dynamic modulus of elasticity. Concurrently, a decrease in the damping ratio was observed for specimens containing GQDs. Additionally, the porosity and chloride penetration depth were lower by 6 % and 30 % for specimens with 0.3 % GQDs. The improvement in workability, mechanical, and dynamic properties of concrete, along with reducing CO2 from the atmosphere, makes GQDs an ideal eco-friendly material. This study is the first to open new pathways for the development of construction materials that are not only structurally superior but also environmentally responsible, marking a significant step forward in the field of civil engineering materials, especially in railway applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Carbon dioxide emissions
Concrete
Dynamic modulus of elasticity
Mechanical properties
Embodied carbon
Concrete sleepers

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express their gratitude to the Second Century Fund (C2F) for the postdoctoral researcher fellowship at Chulalongkorn University. This project is supported by Program Management Unit for Competitiveness (PMUC) Project No. C06F670025. This research is also funded by Thailand Science Research and Innovation Fund Chulalongkorn University. The authors thank CrystalLyte Co., Ltd., for providing the graphene quantum dots.

The authors express their gratitude to the Second Century Fund (C2F) for the postdoctoral researcher fellowship at Chulalongkorn University. This project is supported by Program Management Unit for Competitiveness (PMUC) Project No. C06F670025. This research is also funded by Thailand Science Research and Innovation Fund Chulalongkorn University. The authors thank CrystalLyte Co. Ltd. for providing the graphene quantum dots.

row.entry: Diameter of concrete in case of collapsible slump (mm)
Carbon savings/100 km of railway track (kg)

head.ce:title: Effect of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) on the mechanical, dynamic, and durability properties of concrete

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Addressing ecological concerns stemming from concrete usage is paramount, prompting exploration into additives for mitigation. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been recognized for their potential to improve the mechanical attributes of cement composites. Additionally, the electrochemical reduction of a saturated solution of carbon dioxide (CO2) and monoethanolamine (CO2-MEA) effectively removes CO2 from the atmosphere. This study delves into the influence of GQDs on the mechanical and durability aspects of concrete. It is noted that the control mix of concrete without GQDs was specifically designed for concrete railway sleepers. Varied proportions of GQDs, ranging from 0.3 % to 1.2 % at 0.3 % increments, were incorporated to assess their impact on fresh and hardened concrete properties. Mechanical properties such as compressive strength, splitting tensile strength, flexural strength, and dynamic properties were evaluated. Durability assessments encompassing water absorption and chloride ion penetration were conducted. Microstructural analysis via Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) imaging and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elucidated the concrete's internal composition. Notably, an optimal GQDs percentage of 0.3 % was observed, signifying its efficacy in enhancing the performance of concrete. When 0.3 % of GQDs was added to concrete, compressive strength, split tensile strength, and flexural strength were enhanced by 10.8 %, 23 %, and 11 % respectively. The incorporation of GQDs resulted in a notable improvement in the fundamental frequencies and dynamic modulus of elasticity. Concurrently, a decrease in the damping ratio was observed for specimens containing GQDs. Additionally, the porosity and chloride penetration depth were lower by 6 % and 30 % for specimens with 0.3 % GQDs. The improvement in workability, mechanical, and dynamic properties of concrete, along with reducing CO2 from the atmosphere, makes GQDs an ideal eco-friendly material. This study is the first to open new pathways for the development of construction materials that are not only structurally superior but also environmentally responsible, marking a significant step forward in the field of civil engineering materials, especially in railway applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cement concrete, standing at number one in the list of the most consumed materials for many decades, is central to the construction industry due to its strength, adaptability, and cost-effectiveness [1,2]. Its composition primarily involves cement, aggregates like sand and gravel, and water. Concretes widespread use in construction of residential complexes to large-scale infrastructure underscores its essential role in modern construction. However, the production of concrete, and particularly its cement component, raises significant environmental concerns [3,4]. The process of manufacturing cement includes the calcination of limestone, leading to substantial carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions. These emissions position the cement industry as one of the major contributors to global pollution, making it a significant factor in discussions about climate change. Balancing the demand for durable infrastructure with the urgent need to reduce environmental damage and pollution remains a daunting challenge [5,6]. Significant effort has been put in by researchers to find a sustainable solution to the mentioned problem. One of the means to address this challenge and ensure sustainable development is by reducing the cement content [7–11]. Additives added to concrete to enhance performance help in this regard. Supplementary cementitious materials and other additives have been added to regulate the cement content without compromising its performance. Utilising various components of nanotechnology have the potential to enhance the mechanical properties, durability, and sustainability of concrete, all while minimising its environmental footprint. The incorporation of nanoparticles into concrete has been noted to improve concretes properties, including augmented mechanical strength and durability [7,8,12–20].

Qu et al. [21] investigated the effects of adding 0.08 % carbon dots (CDs) to cement-based composites, revealing a significant 16.8 % increase in compressive strength after 28 days of curing. The researchers observed that CDs, with their small nanometre size and good water solubility, improved the compactness of the cement matrix by filling pores and acting as nucleation sites for hydration products. Additionally, hydrophilic groups like carboxyl and amide on the surface of CDs facilitated strong adsorption on cement particles, further enhancing compressive strength. They suggested that the cost-effective synthesis of CDs makes them a practical alternative nanomaterial for industrial applications, showing potential for enhancing the properties of cement-based composites. Long et al. [22] investigated an approach to enhancing the durability of reinforced concrete structures against chloride-induced corrosion by using carbon dots synthesized through hydrothermal processing and integrated into cement. Specifically, the study found that adding 0.2 % (by weight of cement) of CDs to cement paste increased its chloride binding capacity by 109 % after 14 days in a 3 mol/L NaCl solution. This enhancement was due to the CDs promoting the formation of calcium silicate hydrate (C–S–H) gels and Friedel's salt, which together increased the physical and chemical binding of chloride ions. Long et al. [23] showed that CDs, created via a microwave method, significantly enhanced the performance of cement composites by reducing early hydration heat, improving chloride binding, and boosting mechanical strength. Specifically, adding 0.2 % (by weight of cement) CDs lowered the hydration peak by 75 % and increased chloride binding by 51 %, while 0.1 % (by weight of cement) CDs raised compressive and flexural strength by 17 % and 21 %. CDs achieved these improvements by forming a protective barrier, aiding in the formation of beneficial compounds, and counteracting air-entrainment effects. Shan et al. [24] investigated the potential of CDs to enhance the chloride binding capacity of cement, aiming to extend the service life of reinforced concrete structures by systematically studying the size-induced effect on chloride binding using CDs of varying sizes. They controlled the preparation of three types of CDs with different sizes and confirmed their impact on chloride binding capacity through equilibrium tests. Results showed that CDs with smaller sizes significantly increased chloride binding capacity, with average sizes of 1.73, 5.79, and 12.37 nm enhancing it by 41 %, 20 %, and 14 %, respectively, compared to the blank group after 28 days in a 3 mol/L NaCl solution. He et al. [25] carried out a study in which CDs of three different sizes were synthesized from citric acid, a cheap and non-toxic raw material, and their impact on compressive strength was assessed. Results showed that CDs with average sizes of 12.53, 5.86, and 1.81 nm increased mortar compressive strength by 6.6 %, 15.7 %, and 9.4 %, respectively, after 7 days of curing. The compressive strength initially increased and then decreased as the size of CDs decreased from 12.5 to 1.81 nm. This trend was attributed to the combined effects of CDs-induced nucleation and air entrainment, where smaller CDs enhanced cement hydration but also introduced harmful pores.

One of the recent advancements in nano technology is the use of quantum dots in various applications [26]. Among these Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) have found applications in cement composite world [27]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanoscale carbon particles consisting of one or a few graphene layers, recognized for their unique luminescent properties and zero-dimensional structure [28,29]. GQDs have hydrophilic properties that make them water-soluble and well-dispersed in aqueous solutions, which is advantageous for applications involving water interactions [30,31]. Use of GQDs in cement composites have resulted in enhanced strength properties and workability. He et al. [32] revealed that the incorporation of 0.2 % N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) substantially improved the chloride binding capacity of cement paste by 134 %, mainly through physical adsorption of chloride ions. Additionally, N-GQDs were observed to contribute to a minor increase in chemical binding, significantly enhancing the cement's resistance to chloride corrosion. These findings underscore the potential of N-GQDs in fortifying the durability of reinforced concrete structures.

In 2024, Win et al. [30] conducted a study exploring the effects of powdered supra-graphene quantum dots (supra-GQDs) on the mechanical properties of cement composites. Their findings revealed a significant enhancement in both compressive and flexural strengths of the composites with the addition of supra-GQDs. Moreover, they identified a microstructural bridging mechanism that led to a refined pore structure within the cement matrix. Compared to using GQDs in solution, the utilization of supra-GQDs resulted in a remarkable 30 % reduction in total pore volume. Additionally, Win et al. [31] highlighted the positive effects of GQDs on various properties of cement composites, including their freshness, physical characteristics, mechanical strength, and resistance to sulphate attack. Notably, composites containing 1.2 % powdered quantum dots exhibited the highest compressive and flexural strengths. Moreover, the electrical and thermal conductivity properties of the composites were observed to increase with the GQDs content. The microstructural analysis revealed a bridging mechanism involving the seeding and crystal growth of the C-S-H phase within the composites.

The studies on cement composites using GQDs puts light on the possibility of using them in concrete for enhancing its strength characteristics. Apart from this positive trait, GQDs could also be seen as ecofriendly as it is produced from CO2 from the environment. Most of the infrastructure projects expose concrete structures to extreme environments. Some of those structures like bridges, railway tracks, and subways are subjected to vibrations due to one reason or the other. The combined effect of vibration and extreme exposure affects the structural health of many structures. For example, the dynamic loading in a railway track subjects it to wear and tear exposing it to severe environment and subsequent deterioration [33]. Under these circumstances, a material with enhanced strength and durability characteristics would prove to be useful. GQDs present themselves as an alternative to be used in such structures owing to their better strength and durability. But, using GQDs in such structures would warrant a study on its dynamic properties. Dynamic modulus of elasticity, dynamic Poisson’s ratio, fundamental frequencies and damping ratio are essential dynamic characteristics [34]. Determining fundamental frequencies are linked to avoidance of resonance in transverse, longitudinal and torsional directions [35]. ASTM C-215 has formed the basis of non-destructive studies on dynamic properties of various forms of concrete like foam concrete and fibre reinforced concrete [36,37]. It should also be noted that most of the research conducted with GQDs has focused on cement composite mortars. The exploration of GQDs within concrete has never been investigated. This study envisages a novel attempt to determine the effect of liquid GQDs on the mechanical properties and durability parameters of concrete. The dynamic properties are also examined to assess their suitability for concrete structures susceptible to vibration like railway tracks, subways, bridge, and foundations in earthquake resistant areas etc. The reference parameters used to compare the strength and durability characteristics used in this study are derived from the standards set for prestressed railway sleepers as they are subjected to dynamic loads and long-term degradation [38–40]. In this study, the percentage of liquid GQDs used is 0–1.2 % with an increment of 0.3 % by weight of cement used in the mix. The proportions of materials in the concrete mix were determined based on extensive studies. The mix was designed to be used in structures subjected to dynamic loads with prestressed concrete railway sleepers as a reference. The mechanical properties of concrete namely compressive strength, split tensile strength, and flexural strength were determined. The microstructural analysis of the material was also carried out using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) imaging and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The dynamic properties of concrete were also determined to assess its dynamic performance when used in structures subjected to dynamic forces, such as traffic, earthquakes, blasts, and vehicular traffic. The embodied carbon of the material was assumedly calculated as a case study to be applied in prestressed concrete railway sleepers to ascertain its ecological effectiveness.

Hydraulic cement adhering to ASTM C 1157 [41] was utilized in the research. Using a low carbon cement like hydraulic cement helps to reduce the impact on the ecological balance when compared to ordinary Portland cement (OPC). The fine aggregate consisted of carefully graded natural sand conforming to ASTM C33 standard [42], with a specific gravity of 2.62. Fig. 1 represents the particle size distribution curve of fine aggregate. Coarse aggregate comprised of crushed stones with a maximum nominal size of 20 mm and a specific gravity of 2.65. To improve the workability of fresh mixtures, a modified polycarboxylate-based superplasticizer manufactured by Sika in Thailand was employed. This superplasticizer (light brown liquid, chloride-free) possessed a pH range of 4.5–6, as well as a density of 1060 kg/m³.

In the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), electrochemical reduction of a saturated CO2-MEA (monoethanolamine) solution served as the cornerstone. This process unfolded within a tank reactor, precisely dimensioned at 120×170×160 mm, containing approximately 2 litres of the CO2-MEA solution. During each operational cycle, four pairs of Ag electrodes, deposited on Cu foils and Pt foils, were employed as cathodes and anodes, respectively. The reaction was executed under ambient conditions, maintaining a cell voltage of −2.6 V for a duration of 12 minutes. This cycle repetition occurred ten times, necessitating the replacement of the cathode after each iteration. Following the completion of the ten cycles, the solution underwent a series of post-processing steps. These meticulous procedures culminated in the production of high-quality quantum dots, poised for application in various advanced technological domains (Patent No. WO2022185098A1 [30]). The density of MEA solution containing GQDs used in the study was 1050 kg/m3. The GQDs solution is hydrophilic and homogeneously mixed with concrete [31,32]. The GQDs used is shown in Fig. 2.

The mix proportions are provided in Table 1. The five mixture ratios incorporate varying amounts of liquid GQDs, ranging from 0 % to 1.2 % by weight of cement, with an increment of 0.3 % as additives to the concrete. The control mix, designated as GQDs0, was specifically formulated for railway concrete sleepers, ensuring a compressive strength of at least 55 MPa. In this study, the total volume of water and quantum dot was kept a constant in the material matrix. Hence as the volume of GQDs was increased, the volume of water was reduced accordingly.

Pan mixer was used for mixing concrete. Fine aggregates and coarse aggregates were mixed for 3 minutes in the mixer. After that cement was added into the pan and mixing was carried out for 2 minutes till a uniform mix was obtained. In the control mix, two-thirds of water was added first. A thorough mixing was then carried out for 2 minutes. Then along with one-thirds of water, superplasticizer was added followed by a mixing of 2 minutes. In the case of concrete with GQDs, water was added in 3 stages. First one third of water was added into the mix. After mixing the contents in the pan for 2 minutes, along with one-third of water, superplasticizer was added. GQDs was added to the pan with the remaining water. It was ensured that all the ingredients in the pan mixed thoroughly, and a uniform consistency was achieved. Fig. 3 details the mixing process followed.
The conduct of fresh and hardened properties of concrete are dealt with in this section.
Slump test was conducted to determine the workability of the fresh concrete mixture containing GQDs.

This section details the conduct for the determination of engineering properties of concrete mixes. Compressive strength of the mixes cured for 1, 7, and 28 days was determined using 150 mm cubes as per BS EN 12390–3:2002 [43]. Split tensile strength and flexural strength of concrete cured for 28 days was evaluated using cylinders (diameter 150 mm and height 300 mm) and prisms of dimensions 100 × 100 × 350 mm by adhering to BS EN 12390–6:2009 [44] and BS EN 12390–5:2009 [45] respectively.

The dynamic modulus of elasticity and rigidity of concrete were determined using ASTM C 215 [36]. The fundamental resonant frequencies in transverse, longitudinal and torsional directions were determined using impact resonance method. Concrete prisms of 100 × 100 × 350 mm were used for testing. An IH-02 impact hammer capable of exerting forces up to 2000 N with metal impactor tip was used to excite the vibration. The vibrational response of the specimen was recorded using an accelerometer with a frequency range of up to 45 kHz. This range was adequately suited for capturing the frequencies observed in the specimens tested within this study. Fig. 4 presents the conduct of determination of dynamic modulus of elasticity of prisms. The locations of excitation and response capture for various modes of testing can be identified from Fig. 4.

Eqs. (1) to (4) were utilised for determining the dynamic modulus of elasticity from fundamental longitudinal and transverse frequencies, dynamic modulus of rigidity from fundamental torsional frequency, and dynamic Poisson’s ratio.
(1)
Dynamic modulus of elasticity from fundamental transverse frequency ,
E = CM
n
2
(2)
Dynamic modulus of elasticity from fundamental longitudinal frequency ,
E = DM
n
′
2
(3)
Dynamic modulus of rigidity from fundamental torsional frequency ,
G = BM
n
′ ′
2
(4)
Dynamic Poisson ’ s ratio , μ =
E
2 G
− 1
Where M denotes the mass of prisms,
C = 0.9464
L
3
T
b
t
3
, D =
4 L
bt
, B =
4 LR
A
and L is the length of the specimen, t and b are the dimensions of the specimen (t is the direction in which hammer is driven). T is the correction factor based on the radius of gyration, R is shape factor (1.183 for square prism), A is cross sectional area, and
n , n
′
,
n
′ ′
are the fundamental frequencies in transverse, longitudinal and torsional directions, respectively.

The damping ratio of the specimen was determined using vibration characteristics and the natural frequency obtained during the conduct of impact hammer test. A select band width around the peak amplitude of the first resonance of Frequency Response Function (FRF) curve was used to obtain the damping ratio. Half power band width method was applied on the modal curve for the determination of damping ratio. To check for accuracy of the result obtained, it was cross verified with those obtained from exponential trendline equations for damping from the free vibration response curves. This method directly examines the time decay of the acceleration signals to estimate damping.

The durability assessment was limited to water absorption and chloride ion penetration tests, which are essential for evaluating the long-term durability and performance of concrete. These tests are additional components in the durability assessment of concrete sleepers, according to ISO/DIS 22480–1:2020 (E) [46]. They are particularly relevant for the conditions that railway sleepers are exposed to, especially in environments like those found in tropical climates such as Thailand, where exposure to moisture is frequent.

Water absorption was determined to ensure the durability characteristics of the concrete as per ISO/DIS 22480–1:2020 (E) [46]. Concrete cylinders of diameter 100 mm and height 100 mm were used for the determination of water absorption. Concrete samples were first dried at (105 ± 2) °C for 48 hours until they reached a stable mass, M1. Subsequently, these dried samples were placed in a container with drinking water, with the water level reaching halfway up the samples. After 24 hours, the water level was gently increased to 5 mm above the samples. Following 48 hours of submersion, the samples' hydrostatic weight, M2, was measured. Finally, after removing surface water, the samples were weighed again to determine their saturated weight, M3. The porosity (Vp) is determined using Eq. (4) . The maximum permissible value of porosity is 12 %.
(4)
V
p
=
(
M
3
−
M
1
)
(
M
3
−
M
2
)
× 100

Chloride ion penetration of concrete was determined to ensure the durability characteristics of the concrete required in ISO/DIS 22480–1:2020 (E) [46]. The chloride ion penetration test was carried out based on NT BUILD 492 [47]. Concrete cylinders with diameters of 100 mm and height of 50 mm were cut from cylinders with diameter of 100 mm and length of 200 mm. The preconditioning of specimens involved several steps: after sawing, any burrs were removed from the surfaces using a brush, followed by washing away debris and wiping off excess water. Once the specimens were surface-dry, they were placed in a vacuum container with both end surfaces exposed. The absolute pressure in the container was then reduced to a range of 10–50 mbar (1–5 kPa) within a few minutes and maintained for three hours. Subsequently, while the vacuum pump was still running, the container was filled with a saturated solution of calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2), prepared by dissolving an excess of Ca(OH)2 in distilled water, to fully immerse all specimens. The vacuum was maintained for an additional hour before allowing air to re-enter the container. Finally, the specimens were immersed in the solution for 18 ± 2 hours. The catholyte solution consisted of 10 % NaCl by mass in tap water, while the anolyte solution comprised 0.3 N NaOH dissolved in de-ionized water. A line of silicone sealant was applied around the edges of specimen to enhance tightness to prevent leakage of fluids. The specimen was then placed on the plastic support in the catholyte reservoir. The sleeve above the specimen was filled with 300 ml of anolyte solution. The anode was immersed in the anolyte solution. Finally, the cathode was connected to the negative pole and the anode to the positive pole of the power supply. A voltage of 10 V was applied for 6 hours. The specimen was split axially into two pieces, with the piece having the split section more nearly perpendicular to the end surfaces chosen for penetration depth measurement. Following this, a 0.1 M silver nitrate solution was sprayed onto the freshly split section. After approximately 15 minutes, when the white silver chloride precipitation on the split surface became clearly visible, the penetration depth was measured. Using a slide calliper and a suitable ruler, measurements were taken from the centre to both edges at intervals of 10 mm to obtain seven depths. Fig. 5 presents the testing of chloride ion penetration. Eq. (5) was used for the determination of non-steady state migration coefficient.
(5)
D
nssm
=
0.0239
273 + T
L
U − 2
t
x
d
− 0.0238
273 + T
L
x
d
U − 2
where
D
nssm
is the non-steady state migration coefficient (× 10−12 m2/s), U is the absolute value of applied voltage (V), T is the average value of initial and final temperature in anolyte solution (°C), L is the thickness of specimen (mm),
x
d
is the average value of penetration depth (mm), and t is the testing duration (hours).

Microstructural analysis assists in studying the causes for the behavioural trends in tests carried out on the hardened specimens. FESEM and EDS imaging provide an insight into the internal changes in specimen at a microscopic level. Specimen for FESEM and EDS were collected during the 28-day tests on concrete mixes. The FESEM were observed at 10,000x magnification level.

This section presents the results obtained during the conduct of studies on the effect of liquid GQDs on the mechanical, dynamic and durability characteristics of concrete. The results of microstructural analysis can also be found within this section.

When slump test was done on the mixes using slump cone, the fluidity of the mix increased as the percentage of GQDs was increased. Table 2 presents the results of slump test carried out on the mixes. Fig. 6 presents the slump obtained for various mixes. It can be noted from Table 2 and Fig. 6 that as the percentage of GQDs increased the fluidity of the mix increased. For control mix a slump of 150 mm was observed. Addition of GQDs enhanced the fluidity of the mix resulting in collapsible slump. Since the slump of GQDs mixes collapsed and spread horizontally, their diameter was measured. Increase in percentage of GQDs resulted in increased diameter of spread. The seamless integration of liquid GQDs with the superplasticiser may have assisted in the improvement in workability. The main advantage of utilizing a liquid additive like GQDs was its effectiveness in assisting workability, as the issue of dispersion or tendency of agglomeration was minimal owing to its liquid nature in comparison to powdered additives. The smooth interaction of liquid GQDs with the water due to its hydrophilic nature essentially meant a higher water binding ratio than the water binding ratio of the control mix. Thus, the hydrophilic behaviour of GQDs induced a higher fluidic interaction with the binder aggregate matrix compared to that in the control mix.
The results of mechanical properties of the concrete namely compressive strength, split tensile strength and flexural strength are presented in this section.

Fig. 7 depicts the results of compression tests on cubes. It can be noted that the compressive strength of the concrete with liquid GQDs was higher than that of the control mix. The interaction of GQDs with the material matrix and the successive hydration process yielded in C-S-H gel formation followed by a white crystalline precipitate which occupied the gaps inherent in the material orientation as discussed in Section 3.5. This process proved effective when the GQDs percentage was 0.3 %. On the other hand, using less than the recommended dosage of GQDs can block nucleation sites, slow down hydration kinetics, and lead to a less dense microstructure. These effects ultimately slow down strength development. An increased concentration of GQDs may have resulted in excess carbon dioxide in the material environment posing the risk of carbonation and delayed ettringite formation (Section 3.5.1). Hence, the increase in compressive strength was notably high for concrete containing 0.3 % GQDs, exhibiting enhancements of 15 %, 25 %, and 10 % in 1-day, 7-day, and 28-day strengths, respectively, compared to those of the GQDs0 specimen. However, when the percentage of GQDs was increased beyond 0.3 %, a reduction in compressive strength was noticed. The least improvement in compressive strength was noted for GQDs1.2 specimens. Additionally, it can be seen that the improvement was higher in early strength (1 day and 7 days) for all the mixes predominantly owing to the ability of the hydraulic cement used to facilitate high early strength gain at 1 and 7 days. This may have occurred due to higher amounts of tri-calcium silicate in the material matrix. This trend resulted in a 28-day strength that was close to the 7-day value for GQDs0.3. Consequently, the upper value of the error bar for the GQDs0.3 mix at 7 days was above the lower value of the same mix at 28 days.

Fig. 8 shows the results of split tensile strength. The trend in the results of split tensile strength of concrete with liquid GQDs was on par with that of the compression test. The magnitude of split tensile strength was maximum for concrete with 0.3 % GQDs, showing an improvement of 23 % compared to GQDs0. As explained in the previous section, the effective hydration process aided by strength-enhancing crystalline formations might have been the reason for the enhancement of split tensile strength at a GQDs dosage of 0.3 % [Discussed in Section 3.5 along with microstructure studies]. When the quantum dot percentage was further increased, carbonation introduced by excess carbon in the material environment led to a gradual decrease in the split tensile strength of the specimens. It was also noted that the split tensile strength of the GQDs0.9 and GQDs1.2 specimens was almost the same as that of the control specimen GQDs0.

The results of flexural strength are presented in Fig. 9. The optimum magnitude of flexural strength was noted for GQDs0.3. The improvement in flexural strength was approximately 11 % for GQDs0.3 specimen when compared to that of GQDs0 specimen due to the effect of crystalline formations generated by the interaction of GQDs with the material matrix. However, the effect of carbonation due to excess CO2 played its part in keeping the enhancement in the flexural strength of GQDs1.2 specimen negligible.

The results of dynamic modulus of elasticity studies on prisms are presented in this section including the transverse, longitudinal, and torsional fundamental frequencies, dynamic modulus of elasticities, and damping ratio. The sample load curve obtained from impact hammer and sample acceleration response from accelerometer are provided in Fig. 10. The trendlines representing the maximum and minimum acceleration values at different time intervals are presented to assess the damping characteristics. The FRF curves for transverse, longitudinal and torsional excitations on concrete prisms are provided in Fig. 11.

The fundamental frequencies obtained can be seen from Fig. 12. The fundamental frequencies were identified with the frequency of the first peak of FRF curves. Consequently, the highest fundamental frequency was noted for GQDs0.3 (an improvement of 5 % over GQDs0) in all directions of excitation. These higher fundamental frequencies might have been due to its lower damping characteristics. However, it could be understood that the fundamental frequency of GQDs0.3 was within 5 % of GQDs0. This signified that the dynamic characteristics of GQDs0.3 were similar to those of GQDs0.

Dynamic moduli of the specimens from transverse, longitudinal and torsional frequencies are presented in Fig. 13. The magnitude of dynamic moduli was highest for GQDs0.3 (10 % higher than GQDs0) in all directions. For all other specimen with GQDs, the dynamic moduli was like that of the control mix. The higher dynamic moduli for GQDs0.3 can be directly corelated to their higher fundamental frequency. The trends in dynamic moduli followed the pattern of strength characteristics. The reasons for the trend might have been due to the effect of GQDs on the material matrix. The occupation of inherent pores in the material matrix by the calcium precipitates improved the rigidity of the material matrix. This resulted in enhanced dynamic moduli. However, the material composition may have been affected by the induced carbonation when the GQDs concentration increased beyond 0.3 %. As a result of the voids introduced by this process, the material stiffness reduced. Therefore, the dynamic moduli were lower for specimens with higher GQDs concentration compared to GQDs0.3.

Fig. 14 presents the results of dynamic Poisson's ratio of the mixes. It can be noted that the dynamic Poisson’s ratio of GQDs0.3 was lower than that of GQDs0. The value of dynamic Poisson’s ratio for GQDs0.6 was mostly the same as that of GQDs0.3. When GQDs concentration was increased beyond 0.6 % the dynamic Poisson’s ratio was greater than that of control concrete. The lower number of voids in GQDs0.3 and GQDs0.6 possibly contributed to the reduction in Poisson’s ratio compared to GQDs0. The delayed ettringite formation, resulting in increased pores, might have reduced the overall stiffness of the material matrix. This could have resulted in a higher Poisson’s ratio for GQDs0.9 and GQDs1.2 compared to GQDs0.

The damping ratios of the specimen are presented in Fig. 15. The trends of variation of damping ratio were opposite to that of dynamic modulus of elasticity. As for the case of GQDs0.3 sample, which demonstrated superior mechanical properties, the lowest damping ratio was observed, being approximately 20 % lower than GQDs0 in longitudinal and torsional directions and 8.42 % lower in transverse direction. It is notable that nearly all samples containing GQDs exhibited a damping ratio lower than the control sample without GQDs, with the exception of GQDs1.2 in the transverse direction. A lower damping ratio is associated with higher oscillation at resonance. However, the damping ratio of GQDs0.3 in transverse direction (which is the direction of maximum amplitude of vibration in case of railway sleepers) was within 5 % (4.57 %), which can reduce the effect of large resonant vibrations. Hence, from point of view of application in railway sleepers, it can be understood that the vibration characteristics of GQDs0.3 are well within the standards.
This section details the results of water absorption and chloride ion penetration test.

Fig. 16 shows the results of water absorption test. The porosity was minimum in GQDs0.3 specimen. The percentage of pores increased gradually with increase in GQDs. This indicates that when GQDs0.3 were added to concrete, their interaction with the hydration process and the constituent concrete matrix might have resulted in the formation of crystalline precipitates (as discussed in Section 3.5), which occupied the available pores. This could have reduced the porosity of the GQDs0.3 specimen. The presence of excess carbon dioxide might have paved the way for carbonation and could have led to delayed ettringite formations. This possibly increased the volume of pores in concrete with greater GQDs concentration. Note that the maximum limit of porosity is 12 % as per [46]. The porosity of GQDs0.9 and GQDs1.2 exceeded the permissible limit. So, with respect the volume of voids in the specimen GQDs0.3 performed better among the materials studied.

The optimum percentage of GQDs for enhancing mechanical and dynamic properties was found to be 0.3 %. Consequently, only GQDs0, GQDs0.3, and GQDs1.2 specimens were selected for the chloride ion penetration test. The chloride ion penetration test on the GQDs1.2 specimen was first carried out to assess the effect of maximum percentage of GQDs. During the depth penetration measurements, if the penetration front slated for measurement encountered obstruction by the aggregate, the measurement was relocated to the nearest front where aggregate interference was insignificant. Alternatively, if there were more than five valid depths, this depth was disregarded. Any significant defect in the specimen resulting in a penetration front significantly larger than the average was disregarded. To mitigate the edge effect arising from non-uniform saturation levels or potential leakage, depth measurements were abstained from within approximately 10 mm of the specimen's edge. Fig. 17 presents the result of chloride ion penetration test. Maximum and minimum depth of penetration were observed for GQDs1.2 and GQDs0.3 specimen, respectively. The average chloride ion penetration depth for GQDs0.3 specimen was 30 % lower than that of GQDs0 specimen.

As observed in Fig. 17(b), the magnitude of non-steady state migration coefficient for GQDs0 and GQDs1.2 specimens were close to each other. However, the magnitude of non-steady state migration coefficient for GQDs0.3 specimen was considerably lower. This could be attributed to the denser and more closely spaced intrinsic structure of the GQDs0.3 specimen, as explained later in the microstructural analysis. Thus, it could be inferred that the GQDs0.3 specimen offered better resistance to chloride ion penetration compared to the GQDs0 specimen.

This section deals with the microstructural imaging of GQDs0, GQDs0.3, and GQDs1.2 specimens. These studies were limited to GQDs0, GQDs0.3, and GQDs1.2 specimens as they represent specimens with the minimum, maximum, and optimum percentage of GQDs.

The FESEM images for concrete with GQDs percentage of 0.3 % and 1.2 % along with control concrete has been given in Fig. 18. In concrete with 0.3 % GQDs, additional crystalline formations could be noticed (see Fig. 18(b)). The inherent voids of the concrete were ideally filled with the generated C-S-H gel and crystals formed due to the addition of 0.3 % GQDs. This resulted in better strength and durability characteristics for GQDs0.3 specimens compared to the control concrete. However, as the amount of GQDs was increased beyond 0.3 %, the microstructure images in Fig. 18(c) show the presence of closely spaced needle-shaped precipitates. These needle-shaped precipitates, caused by delayed ettringite formations due to excess carbon dioxide, might have resulted in harmful chemical effects in concrete, paving the way for greater porosity and reduced mechanical properties in specimens GQDs0.6, GQDs0.9, and GQDs1.2. These trends agree well with those obtained by Win et al. [30,31] in their studies on cement.

Fig. 19 and Table 3 presents the results of EDS for concrete with 0 %, 0.3 %, and 1.2 % GQDs. It can be visible from the corresponding elemental composition depicted in Table 3 that the atomic composition of calcium was higher for GQDs0.3 than GQDs0. This could explain the formation of crystalline structures in GQDs0.3. The presence of higher atomic oxygen and lower calcium in GQDs1.2 might have been the reasons for lower strength characteristics in specimens with more than 0.3 % GQDs. Additionally, the presence of a higher concentration of carbon in the GQDs1.2 specimen may have had harmful effects on the strength development of the concrete. This relatively high carbon content could help confirm the formation of delayed ettringite from carbonation in the GQDs1.2 specimen. Another point of interest is that in the GQDs0.3 specimen, the free carbon available was almost nil. The negligible presence of carbon and the higher concentration of calcium in GQDs0.3 may have been the prominent reasons for its better strength and durability performance.

This section presents the embodied carbon values of the materials used for the mixes. The embodied carbon values of GQDs0 and GQDs0.3 are shown in Table 4. Though GQDs addition in concrete paves the way to enhanced mechanical and dynamic characteristics, the ecological benefits need to be explored. An attempt has been made to compare the ecological effectiveness of applying GQDs with OPC and hydraulic cement, as they are the most prominent types of cement in use. The data for embodied carbon of OPC and hydraulic cement is provided by TMCA (Thai Cement Manufacturers Association) [48]. To understand the environmental benefit of utilising GQDs, a comparison of using GQDs0 and GQDs0.3 (as it provides optimum benefits) in railway sleepers is carried out. The dimensions of sleepers and their spacing vary according to track design. So, the carbon emissions for the materials used in construction phase also will vary according to the track design. Hence, for better understanding and to avoid ambiguity, a simple case study of 1 km-ballasted railway track with a concrete sleeper of dimension 0.25 m × 0.15 m × 2.6 m and with a sleeper spacing of 0.6 m (1667 sleepers per kilometre of railway track) is considered to determine the ecological efficacy of liquid GQDs. The CO2 emission of GQDs was assumedly calculated from carbon that is formed in the synthesis process. It is interesting to note that when GQDs was used in concrete with ordinary Portland cement (OPC), for every 100 kilometre of railway track laid, about 15 tonnes of CO2 is removed from the atmosphere (Table 4). Whereas a combination of hydraulic cement and GQDs can remove about 668 tonnes of CO2 from the world for every 100 kilometre of railway track laid when compared to OPC. Thus, GQDs0.3 presents itself as an ecofriendly additive with hydraulic cement, reducing significant quantity of CO2 from the atmosphere when compared to the use of concrete with OPC. It can also be pointed out that as strength gain can be achieved by adding GQDs, an optimisation study can be carried out to reduce the amount of binder used to attain the required strength. This could further reduce the ecological impact of using concrete with GQDs.

The effect of utilising liquid GQDs as part of the concrete was assessed by varying the percentage of GQDs to up to 1.2 %. The mechanical properties of concrete namely compressive strength, split tensile strength, flexural strength, dynamic modulus of elasticity, and damping ratio of concrete were evaluated. Determination of durability properties including water absorption and chloride ion penetration was done. Microstructural studies were done using FESEM and EDS to justify the trends in properties. The major conclusions from the study are as follows.
•
The compressive strength of concrete increases with increase in the percentage of GQDs due to the formation of C-S-H gel and crystalline formations within the material matrix. A maximum improvement of 10.8 % was noted in the 28-day compressive strength for specimens with a GQDs percentage of 0.3 %. However, the compressive strength continuously decreased when the percentage of GQDs increased beyond 0.3 % due to the effect of carbonation and delayed ettringite formation.
•
The trends in the variation of split tensile strength and flexural strength were similar to that of compressive strength. The split tensile strength and flexural strength of specimens with 0.3 % GQDs increased by 23 % and 11 %, respectively, compared to specimens without GQDs.
•
The magnitude of the fundamental frequency for longitudinal, torsional, and transverse excitation of concrete with 0.3 % GQDs was enhanced by 5 % compared to the control specimen. Additionally, the improvement in dynamic moduli for the transverse, longitudinal, and torsional frequencies of concrete with 0.3 % GQDs was 10 %. The variation in the damping ratio was opposite to the trend of variation for frequencies. The damping ratio of the GQDs0.3 specimen was 20 % lower than that of the GQDs0 specimen.
•
The results of porosity of mixes indicate that for a GQDs percentage of 0.3, the porosity is 6 % lower than that of control concrete. But, as the quantity of GQDs is increased the porosity increases.
•
The chloride ion penetration test indicated that the concrete with 0.3 % GQDs has a 30 % lower average penetration depth than GQDs0. Meanwhile, the average chloride ion penetration depth was almost the same for the control specimen and the specimen with 1.2 % GQDs.
•
The microstructural investigations of the specimens indicate that C-S-H gel formation increased with the addition of 0.3 % GQDs. However, when the GQDs percentage was 1.2 %, the formation of delayed ettringite and inherent voids inside the concrete mix resulted in the reduction of strength properties and durability.
•
A case study is showcased, revealing that the integration of GQDs with hydraulic cement in railway sleepers significantly contributes to environmental sustainability. The embodied carbon estimates indicate that when GQDs are used with hydraulic cement in railway sleepers, about 668 tonnes of CO2 can be removed from atmosphere for every 100 kilometres of track laid.

Overall, the results of the study indicate that the optimum percentage of GQDs for concrete is 0.3 %, as it can provide beneficial results in workability, strength, and durability parameters. The positive effect of the optimum dosage of GQDs on the mechanical and durability properties of concrete, along with its ecological benefits, instils confidence for detailed investigations on product-based properties to establish its effectiveness. This study not only showcases a novel application but also sets a precedent for integrating advanced nanomaterials into construction practices, promoting both technological advancement and ecological responsibility.

Papassara Yamkasikorn: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ratabhat Wangtawesap: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Anand Raj: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Joongjai Panpranot: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Sakdirat Kaewunruen: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources. Pitcha Jongvivatsakul: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology. Lapyote Prasittisopin: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Conceptualization. Thwe Thwe Win: Validation, Resources, Formal analysis. Chayut Ngamkhanong: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their gratitude to the Second Century Fund (C2F) for the postdoctoral researcher fellowship at Chulalongkorn University. This project is supported by Program Management Unit for Competitiveness (PMUC) Project No. C06F670025. This research is also funded by Thailand Science Research and Innovation Fund Chulalongkorn University. The authors thank CrystalLyte Co., Ltd., for providing the graphene quantum dots.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Innovative GQDs and supra-GQDs assemblies for developing high strength and conductive cement composites
